agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A courier-tracking feature where customers can view their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have submitted directly contradictory requirements — Customer Success demands full GPS transparency, Operations demands zero location visibility — each claiming their requirement is non-negotiable.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Tracking / Last-Mile Visibility",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Courier", "definition": "The driver or delivery person physically transporting a package to the customer." },
      { "term": "Real-time location", "definition": "The courier's GPS-derived position, updated at a frequent interval during an active delivery." },
      { "term": "Active delivery", "definition": "The window of time between a courier picking up a package and completing its delivery to the customer." },
      { "term": "Location visibility", "definition": "The degree to which a courier's position is exposed to the end customer — ranging from exact coordinates to no visibility at all." },
      { "term": "ETA", "definition": "Estimated time of arrival — a derived value that can communicate delivery progress without exposing raw location." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Courier is stationary for an extended period (e.g., traffic, break) and customer sees no movement", "expected_behavior": "Depends on resolution — exact GPS makes this anxiety-inducing for customer and surveillance-like for courier; abstracted progress indicators mitigate both." },
    { "scenario": "Courier deviates from expected route (multi-drop, personal errand)", "expected_behavior": "Exact GPS exposes this to customer, creating complaints; abstracted view hides it but may mask genuine delivery problems." },
    { "scenario": "Courier is near delivery address but serving another customer first", "expected_behavior": "Exact GPS shows courier 'circling' the area without delivering, generating support tickets. Abstracted ETA/status handles this gracefully." },
    { "scenario": "Customer is not home and needs to gauge when to return", "expected_behavior": "This is the legitimate customer need — can be served by ETA countdown or proximity zone rather than exact coordinates." },
    { "scenario": "Courier safety incident — location data subpoenaed or misused", "expected_behavior": "Exact GPS shared with customers creates a data trail with privacy and legal implications for the courier." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Driver churn is extremely expensive in logistics — recruiting, onboarding, and route-learning costs per driver far exceed the marginal revenue from a tracking feature. If Operations is correct that drivers are leaving over surveillance, the cost of exact GPS tracking likely exceeds its revenue benefit. Conversely, Customer Success may have data showing tracking drives conversion/retention — but this needs to be validated against driver acquisition costs.",
    "operational": "Losing experienced couriers degrades delivery quality, increases failed deliveries, and raises costs across the entire operation. A courier shortage is an existential operational risk in last-mile logistics. Tracking is a nice-to-have; having drivers is not.",
    "user": "Customers want predictability and control — knowing *when* their package arrives, not necessarily *where* the driver is at every second. The actual customer job-to-be-done is likely 'reduce uncertainty about delivery time,' which can be solved without exact GPS. Couriers are also users of this system — their experience and sense of dignity directly affects service quality.",
    "risk": "HIGH. Shipping either requirement as stated will damage the business. Exact GPS loses drivers. Zero visibility loses the competitive differentiation Customer Success is banking on. There are also privacy/GDPR/labor-law risks in sharing worker location data with third parties without consent frameworks. This is a two-sided marketplace problem — optimizing for one side at the expense of the other collapses the marketplace.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Want delivery predictability. Exact GPS is one way to provide it, but not the only way. Over-indexing on GPS may actually increase anxiety (watching a dot not move)." },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "Directly affected by surveillance concerns. Driver retention is existential for the business. Their consent and comfort must be factored in — they are not just resources, they are a scarce labor market." },
      { "group": "Customer Success team", "impact": "Owns customer satisfaction metrics. Needs a tracking feature to differentiate, but may be anchored on 'exact GPS' when the actual customer need is broader." },
      { "group": "Operations team", "impact": "Owns driver retention and fleet health. Raising a legitimate labor-market concern that has direct P&L impact." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "NOT YET CONSULTED but must be. Sharing worker GPS data with customers has privacy and labor-law implications in many jurisdictions (GDPR, CCPA, emerging gig-worker protection laws)." },
      { "group": "Executive leadership", "impact": "Must arbitrate this conflict. This is a strategic business decision, not a feature decision — it defines the company's stance on its two-sided marketplace." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "Two non-negotiable requirements from peer-level stakeholders are in direct, irreconcilable contradiction. Neither team has authority to override the other. No executive decision or policy arbitration has been provided. Additionally, Legal/Compliance has not weighed in on what is even permissible. The spec as given is unshippable — any implementation would violate one team's stated hard requirement. Confidence is very low because there is no valid spec to implement against.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "The contradiction is real and fundamental — not a misunderstanding that can be resolved by reading the requirements more carefully",
      "The actual customer job-to-be-done is delivery predictability, not raw GPS surveillance",
      "Driver retention is a legitimate and high-stakes operational concern in last-mile logistics",
      "A middle-ground solution likely exists (abstracted tracking, ETA-based visibility, proximity zones) but has not been authorized by either stakeholder"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Which stakeholder team has decision authority to break this deadlock",
      "Whether Customer Success has data proving exact GPS drives conversion vs. whether any tracking feature would suffice",
      "Whether Operations has data proving GPS tracking specifically causes driver churn vs. other retention factors",
      "Legal and regulatory constraints on sharing courier location data with customers",
      "Whether couriers could opt in to location sharing (making it consent-based) as a compromise"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Both teams are acting in good faith and representing real business concerns",
      "Neither team has unilateral authority to override the other",
      "No executive decision has been made to prioritize one side over the other",
      "Legal/compliance review has not yet occurred"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "Customer Success requires exact GPS visibility for customers ('at all times during delivery'). Operations requires zero GPS visibility for customers ('must never be shown'). These are mutually exclusive as stated. Both teams claim their requirement is non-negotiable. There is no valid implementation that satisfies both requirements simultaneously.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Couriers", "Customer Success", "Operations", "Legal/Compliance", "Executive Leadership"],
      "options": [
        "A: Implement exact GPS tracking per Customer Success — accept driver churn risk",
        "B: Implement zero tracking per Operations — accept loss of competitive differentiator",
        "C: Executive arbitration to define a middle-ground policy (e.g., abstracted proximity zones, ETA-only, consent-based opt-in by couriers) — requires a decision-maker above both teams",
        "D: Run a controlled experiment — A/B test abstracted tracking vs. exact GPS vs. no tracking, measuring both customer satisfaction AND driver retention simultaneously"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C, informed by Option D. An executive sponsor must arbitrate this conflict — it is a business strategy decision, not a feature spec. I strongly recommend a middle-ground approach: show customers an abstracted delivery progress indicator (ETA, proximity zone like 'Your courier is 3 stops away') rather than exact GPS. This serves the customer's actual job-to-be-done (reducing delivery uncertainty) without subjecting couriers to real-time surveillance. A controlled experiment can validate this hypothesis before full rollout. Legal/Compliance must also review any solution before implementation. DO NOT proceed to implementation until this is resolved."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Legal and regulatory review of sharing courier GPS data with customers has not been conducted. In many jurisdictions (EU/GDPR, California/CCPA, emerging gig-worker protection laws), sharing worker location data with third parties requires explicit consent frameworks and may have restrictions. This is a blocker for any solution involving location data.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Couriers", "Executive Leadership"],
      "options": [
        "Engage Legal/Compliance to review permissibility before any design work",
        "Proceed with consent-based design that assumes opt-in will be required"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Engage Legal/Compliance immediately. Their findings may constrain the solution space and render the Customer Success requirement legally impermissible regardless of business preference."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Specification contains irreconcilable contradiction between two peer-level stakeholder teams", "business_rule_broken": "No business rule can be defined when the two authoritative sources demand opposite behaviors. Implementing either requirement as stated guarantees violating the other." },
    { "violation": "No legal/compliance review of worker location data sharing", "business_rule_broken": "Sharing courier GPS coordinates with customers may violate privacy regulations and labor laws. Cannot define acceptance criteria without knowing the legal constraints." }
  ]
}
